item.title: Subtleties of Python
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
In our proôÙfesôÙsion, atôÙtenôÙtion to deôÙtail is of utôÙmost imôÙporôÙtance. Any good ôÙsoftôÙware enôÙgiôÙneer unôÙderôÙstands that deôÙtails matôÙter a lot, as they make the dôÙifôÙferôÙence beôÙtween a workôÙing unit or a disôÙasôÙter [1].

This is why clean code is not just about forôÙmatôÙting or arôÙrangôÙing the code. It's neiôÙther a foible. It is inôÙstead, payôÙing atôÙtenôÙtion exôÙactôÙly to those deôÙtails that will make a big difôÙferôÙence in proôÙducôÙtion.

Let's see some exôÙamôÙples of this in Python.

Read moreãÎ (4 min remaining to read)

item.title: Exploring Generators and Coroutines
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Let's reôÙvisôÙit the idea of genôÙerôÙaôÙtors in Python, in orôÙder to unôÙderôÙstand how the supôÙport for corouôÙtines was achieved in latôÙest verôÙsions of Python (3.6, at the time of this writôÙing).

By reôÙviewôÙing the mileôÙstones on genôÙerôÙaôÙtors, chronoôÙlogôÙiôÙcalôÙly, we can get a betôÙter idea of the evoôÙluôÙtion that lead to asynôÙchroôÙnous proôÙgramôÙming in Python.

We will reôÙview the main changes in Python that reôÙlate to genôÙerôÙaôÙtors and asynôÙchronous proôÙgramôÙming, startôÙing with PEP-255 (SimôÙple GenôÙerôÙaôÙtors), PEP-342 (ôÙCorouôÙtines via EnôÙhanced GenôÙerôÙaôÙtors), PEP-380 (SynôÙtax for delôÙeôÙgatôÙing to a ôÙSubôÙ-ôÙGenôÙerôÙaôÙtor), and finôÙishôÙing with PEP-525 (AsynôÙchronous GenôÙerôÙaôÙtors).

Read moreãÎ (16 min remaining to read)

item.title: Refuse the temptation to pass
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This is an opinôÙion I someôÙtimes reôÙmemôÙber when seeôÙing some code. Don't take it as a strong adôÙvice, or a rule, but inôÙstead as a genôÙerôÙal guideôÙline that might help yôÙou to imôÙprove the code slightôÙly.

On this post I will share a tiny and opinôÙionôÙatôÙed arôÙguôÙment about why there are usuôÙalôÙly betôÙter things to do in the code inôÙstead of just pass.

Read moreãÎ (1 min remaining to read)

This talk was preôÙsentôÙed at PyôÙCon CZ 2017 and EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2017. Here are the refôÙerôÙences and reôÙsources for the preôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion.

The code used for the talk, is availôÙable here and ôÙconôÙtains some exôÙplaôÙnaôÙtions along with the exôÙamôÙples.

Read moreãÎ (1 min remaining to read)

item.title: PyCon CZ - Day 1
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
The day startôÙed at 9.00, and at first it was time of checkôÙ-in, etc. AfôÙter that, I solved one ridôÙdle by kiôÙwi.ôÙcom, and earned a disôÙcount in flightôÙs, which was a nice way to start the conôÙferôÙence.

Then afôÙter breakôÙfast and some netôÙworkôÙing goôÙing through the booths of the sponôÙsors, it was time for the first talk of the day: "When bugs bite - why neôÙglectôÙing edge casôÙes can kill".

It was a great talk, a case for softôÙware enôÙgiôÙneerôÙing in genôÙerôÙal (it's not Python-speciôÙfic, which is what makes the topôÙic more inôÙterôÙestôÙing). The best thing I liked about the talk was the reôÙmarkôÙs, and the idea of shiftôÙing the ôÙmindôÙset when it comes to deôÙvelôÙopôÙmenôÙt, so we should do some "negôÙaôÙtive thinkôÙing" in the form of what can go wrong with this? How can this fail?, and such. It's imôÙporôÙtanôÙt, beôÙcause this is critôÙiôÙcal in orôÙder to make roôÙbust softôÙware. Most probôÙlems I have seen on softôÙware bugs were reôÙlatôÙed to some opôÙtiôÙmistic thinkôÙing, and even worse, opôÙtiôÙmistic unit testôÙing, in the way that deôÙvelôÙopôÙers onôÙly test ôÙfor hapôÙpy-ôÙpath sceôÙnarôÙios, withôÙout thinkôÙing all sort of things that can go wrong.

AfôÙterôÙwardôÙs, was the time for the talk about parôÙalôÙlel proôÙcessôÙing (poor perôÙsonôÙ's ôÙparôÙalôÙlel proôÙcessôÙing), which was fine. Then I spent some time tackôÙling some of the chalôÙlenges sponôÙsors had availôÙable, so I did some codôÙing and reôÙcap of the events so far.

Then I lisôÙtened to a talk about wolfcrypt which is a tool for crypôÙto in Python. The talk inôÙtroôÙduced some crypôÙto conôÙcepts (symôÙmetôÙric crypôÙto, pubôÙlic key, etc.), which was good. Most of the quesôÙtions reôÙvolved around comôÙparôÙiôÙson with othôÙer tools in python (deôÙfault liôÙbraries, pyôÙcrypôÙto, etc.).

Then it was time for lunch and do some more codôÙing, and the next talk I atôÙtendôÙed to was called "should I mock or should I not?", which I liked very ôÙmuch and gave some food for thought.

Then it came one more talk, afôÙter which it was time for mine, so I preôÙsentôÙed clean code in Python. There were some inôÙterôÙestôÙing quesôÙtionôÙs, and the enôÙtire pôÙreôÙsenôÙtaôÙtion went relôÙaôÙtiveôÙly quickôÙly.

Once the talk sesôÙsions were over, there was one last track for lightôÙning talkôÙs, which are alôÙways suôÙper enôÙterôÙtainôÙing.

AfôÙter the finôÙish of the first day, I atôÙtendôÙed the speakôÙer's dinôÙner, which was a nice opôÙporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to netôÙwork with the comôÙmuôÙniôÙty, which is alôÙways great.

LookôÙing forôÙward to some more inôÙterôÙestôÙing talks toôÙmorôÙrow, and to see the reôÙsultôÙs of the chalôÙlenges.
